name: Google Photos
short_description: A cloud photo and video service that uses machine learning to search, organize, and resurface a person's visual memories.
long_description: Launched at Google I/O 2015, Google Photos applies face, object, and scene recognition to a user's entire camera roll so people can search their memories in plain language ('beach', 'dog', a person's name) and have albums, highlights, and edits generated automatically. As a software-only platform riding on the cameras of billions of phones, it is a key example of physical AI in daily life: it does not have a body, but it perceives and interprets the physical world captured by everyday devices and acts as an ambient autobiographical memory aid. Its sensing of faces, places, and scenes also makes it a recurring touchstone in debates over visual privacy and on-by-default machine perception.

images.caption: The Google Photos app icon (2020-2025 design), a four-color pinwheel.
images.alt_text: The Google Photos logo: a pinwheel of four rounded petals in blue, red, yellow, and green arranged around a white center.

sources.title: Google Photos: A new beginning
sources.access_notes: Official launch announcement at Google I/O 2015.

sources.title: Google Photos officially unveiled at Google I/O 2015

interactions.title: Ask your library a whole question
interactions.description: Instead of guessing keywords, type 'show me the best photo from each national park I've visited' and Gemini reads across the library, reasons over places and dates, and answers with the shots themselves.

interactions.title: A highlight reel you never edited
interactions.description: Without being asked, the app stitches the past week or a recurring person into a scored video and slides it to the top of your feed the next morning.

interactions.title: Move the kid, keep the beach
interactions.description: Drag a person to the center of a crowded vacation shot and Magic Editor regenerates the sand and water that were behind them, no eraser strokes required.

interactions.title: Tell it to stop showing you someone
interactions.description: After a death or a breakup, people dig into Memories settings to block a specific face or a single date so the carousel stops surfacing them with cheerful music, without having to delete the pictures.

interactions.title: The library that doesn't know who died
interactions.description: Years after a parent's death, the 'look back at four years today' card queues up images of their final illness set to soothing music — a grief ambush from a system that curates for delight but can't read context.

interactions.title: Snap the Wi-Fi sticker, copy the password
interactions.description: People photograph a router label or a handwritten note, then long-press the recognized text to lift the Wi-Fi password or address straight off the image — using the search index as a pocket OCR scanner.

interactions.title: Find the prescription you forgot you photographed
interactions.description: Ask in plain language for the pill bottle or insurance card you once snapped in a hurry, and the text-in-image search pulls it out of thousands of photos — turning the camera roll into a searchable filing cabinet built from existing OCR and natural-language search.

interactions.title: A gentler year-in-review
interactions.description: Before assembling a recap, the assistant could ask whether the past months were happy ones and quietly route around blocked faces and grief-flagged dates — grounded in the existing hide-people controls and on-device scene understanding, but currently left to manual settings.
